target: WIN-064 detail_evidence, detail_extra, tldr_evidence — azimuthal symmetry attack, Sub-Terrestrial Wall quote verification, depth=378 km category error, live-panel unconditional-pass analysis

patches.replacement_html: The P-wave shadow zone is one of the most powerful pieces of evidence FOR a spherical, layered Earth. Seismic waves refract as they pass through layers of different density. The shadow zone at 104°–140° was first explained by Oldham (1906) and refined by Gutenberg (1913, Physikalische Zeitschrift 14: 1217–1218) as proof that Earth has a liquid outer core. S-waves are completely blocked by the outer core (they cannot propagate through liquid), creating a total S-wave shadow beyond ~104°. P-waves refract around the core and re-emerge as PKP arrivals beyond ~140°, with the shadow zone between these angles. The radius and depth of the liquid core (2,891 km depth, outer core from 2,891–5,150 km) are derived directly from this shadow zone geometry on a SPHERE. On a flat disc 20,015 km in radius, angular distances of 104°–140° (corresponding to 11,600–15,600 km surface distance, using sphere math — which is the point) have no geometric relationship to a layered spherical refraction pattern. Claiming this as evidence for a flat disc is self-defeating: the shadow zone calculations assume spherical wave propagation through concentric spherical layers. The azimuthal-symmetry problem. The dome names a specific mechanism: its WIN-064 entry states that the Sub-Terrestrial Wall of the Closed Toroidal Ovoid creates a physical P-wave shadow zone between 104° and 140° angular distance from any seismic source, corresponding to the toroidal return-path boundary. This mechanism is a fixed geographic feature (the bottom plate of the ovoid cavity). A fixed boundary cannot reproduce the observed geometry. The P-wave shadow zone forms a symmetric ring at 104°–140° around every earthquake, at every azimuth, regardless of where the epicenter sits — Mount Aso yesterday, the Chile Triple Junction today, Tohoku tomorrow. A shadow cast by a single fixed wall would fall in specific directions relative to that wall (east-of-the-wall, west-of-the-wall), not form a concentric ring around each new epicenter worldwide. The standard-model explanation produces observer-centered rings by construction, because the spherical core is concentric with the radial projection of each epicenter. See Part 6 for the broader pattern of observer-centered geometry that any fixed-boundary mechanism inherently fails. The live-power panel is measuring the wrong thing. The dome's monitor surfaces this WIN as Domain 14 on its live-power dashboard with predicted: "Shadow at 104–140°", observed: "Latest deep quake: 15 km SSE of Sant'Angelo, Italy (depth=378.035 km)", tolerance_pct: null, error_pct: null, pass: true (current status_history.json, domain index 14). Three problems in one panel: (1) the observation is a live-fetched USGS record of a recent deep earthquake — its focal depth in kilometres — which has no logical connection to whether P-waves are absent at 104°–140° angular distance from that quake; (2) pass: true is set unconditionally because the observation is never compared to the prediction; (3) the dome's own methodology states Each domain has explicit tolerance bands. If prediction falls outside tolerance, it fails — no exceptions, yet this domain ships with null tolerance. A domain with null tolerance that never actually compares obs to pred is unfalsifiable by the dome's own self-imposed criterion and should not count toward the accuracy denominator.

Preempting the "radially symmetric structure" defense. A dome defender could try to rescue azimuthal symmetry by arguing the Sub-Terrestrial Wall is itself radially symmetric about the disc center — for instance, a concentric annular feature on the bottom plate. This does not help. Symmetry about a single fixed point on the disc is not the same as symmetry about every earthquake's epicenter. A concentric annulus centered on the disc pole would produce shadows at 104°–140° measured from the disc pole — not from every epicenter. For observer-centered rings around every quake worldwide, the sub-terrestrial structure would have to be radially symmetric about a different center for each quake — i.e., there would have to be an infinite family of concentric layered bodies, one per possible epicenter, all simultaneously present. The only geometric object that satisfies "radially symmetric about every surface point simultaneously" is a concentric spherical shell beneath a spherical surface. Conceding this defense is therefore equivalent to conceding the sphere. The more honest dome reply is to drop the "confirmed" label and restate WIN-064 as "disc cosmology is not logically incompatible with shadow zones" — but a model that is merely not-incompatible with an observation makes no prediction, and the dome's own scoreboard counts this as a confirmation, not a non-contradiction.

The P-wave shadow zone (no direct P-waves between 104°–140° from an earthquake) is one of the strongest proofs of a spherical, layered Earth. Crucially it forms a symmetric ring around every earthquake worldwide — a pattern a fixed "Sub-Terrestrial Wall" cannot produce, but concentric spherical layers do naturally. The dome adopts the observation while proposing a mechanism geometrically incompatible with what's observed.

holes_addressed: ISS-1273 (major): azimuthal-symmetry ring attack from Part 6 now appears directly in the WIN-064 detail block with cross-reference; reader of the WIN panel no longer gets the weaker 'assumes a sphere' argument alone.

ISS-1274 (moderate): 'Sub-Terrestrial Wall' phrase verified live on https://john09289.github.io/predictions/wins.html#win-064 — the dome really does use this exact capitalized phrase. The raw-text archive (raw-text/02-wins.txt) did not include the wins.html content, which is why the curmudgeon could not find it. Quote is now attributed with direct link to the dome source so a dome defender cannot strawman-accuse us. The attribution in Part 6 is therefore also vindicated (no change required to sections.json for this item).

ISS-1275 (moderate): 'Observed: ... depth=378.035 km' now quoted verbatim with explicit unit-mismatch explanation. Also upgraded: the observation is live-fetched from USGS FDSN (not static as c3 assumed) but measures focal depth of a deep quake — orthogonal to the 104°–140° angular prediction. pass:true is set unconditionally because obs and pred are never compared. tolerance:null is flagged against the dome's own 'no exceptions' tolerance-band methodology.
new_evidence_added: Verbatim dome quote: 'the Sub-Terrestrial Wall of the Closed Toroidal Ovoid creates a physical P-wave shadow zone between 104° and 140° angular distance from any seismic source' (https://john09289.github.io/predictions/wins.html#win-064, verified 2026-04-21).

Live status_history.json dump of Domain 14 (P-wave Shadow Zone): predicted='Shadow at 104–140°', observed='Latest deep quake: 15 km SSE of Sant'Angelo, Italy (depth=378.035 km)', tolerance_pct=null, error_pct=null, pass=true (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/john09289/predictions/main/docs/data/status_history.json, verified 2026-04-21).

Cross-reference to dome's own methodology text 'If prediction falls outside tolerance, it fails — no exceptions' (raw-text/08-live-power.txt lines 163-174; dome live page https://john09289.github.io/predictions/live.html).

anticipated_objections.objection: The observation is live-fetched, so calling it 'hardcoded' or 'display-only' is inaccurate.
anticipated_objections.response: The OBSERVATION field live-fetches USGS data, but it fetches the wrong thing: the focal depth of a recent deep earthquake, not any measurement of whether P-wave arrivals are absent at 104°–140°. A live fetch of an irrelevant quantity is not a test of the prediction. The PREDICTION field ('Shadow at 104–140°') is a static string; the pass/fail logic never compares obs to pred, so pass:true is set unconditionally. We do not call the domain 'hardcoded' in the prose — we call it unfalsifiable, which it is, by the dome's own tolerance-band methodology.

anticipated_objections.objection: The Sub-Terrestrial Wall could be radially symmetric (e.g., a concentric disc ring), which would preserve azimuthal symmetry.
anticipated_objections.response: Radial symmetry about the DISC CENTER is not the same as radial symmetry about every earthquake's EPICENTER. The observed rings are centered on each epicenter, not on the disc pole. To reproduce observer-centered symmetry worldwide, the sub-terrestrial structure would need to be radially symmetric about every surface point simultaneously — which is the defining property of a concentric spherical shell, i.e., a sphere. Conceding this defense concedes the sphere. (Covered in detail_extra.)

anticipated_objections.objection: WIN-064 is not claimed as 'derived' — it is claimed as a 'confirmed' prediction that is not incompatible with disc cosmology.
anticipated_objections.response: The dome's own scoreboard counts WIN-064 as a CONFIRMED prediction (status: PASS), not as a non-contradiction. A model that is merely not-incompatible with an observation has no predictive content — it cannot be wrong, which by the dome's own Popperian framing means it does not score. If the dome wants to downgrade WIN-064 to 'non-contradictory,' we will accept that concession and the confirmation count drops by one.

anticipated_objections.objection: The 'pass: true' might come from a flag we cannot see rather than from a missing comparison.
anticipated_objections.response: We inspect status_history.json directly: error_pct=null and tolerance_pct=null for this domain, whereas most other domains carry numeric error_pct and tolerance_pct values that drive the pass boolean. A pass that is set without a comparison stage is what 'Tolerance: N/A' reduces to in practice. If the dome is computing pass from some other state, it is welcome to surface the computation; until then, the observation is that the domain passes unconditionally.

kernel_of_truth: Any serious Earth model has to address the P-wave shadow zone — it is the classical proof of the liquid outer core and one of the most widely cited pieces of internal-Earth evidence. The dome (a) acknowledges the phenomenon exists, (b) pins a specific angular range (104°–140°) to it, and (c) proposes a named structural mechanism (the Sub-Terrestrial Wall of the Closed Toroidal Ovoid). Most heterodox cosmologies do not engage with shadow-zone data at all. Taking the observation seriously and naming a candidate mechanism is a real baseline of intellectual seriousness. The claim fails not because the dome ignores the data but because the proposed mechanism is geometrically incompatible with the observer-centered symmetry of the actual observation, and because the dome's own live-power panel never runs the comparison it claims to run.
tldr_review_notes: Current tldr_evidence (54 words) focused on sphere-assuming self-defeat. New tldr (55 words) preserves punchline-first structure but upgrades to the stronger attack: a FIXED wall cannot produce observer-centered symmetric rings around every quake worldwide. tldr_verdict ('The shadow zone proves a spherical, layered Earth — its derivation assumes a sphere. Claiming it as dome evidence is self-defeating.') unchanged — the verdict still holds and the new argument strengthens the existing conclusion.
verdict_text_unchanged_reason: detail_verdict_text unchanged. It already references the correct monitoring tag ('hardcoded' — now consistent with the live-panel static prediction string and the unfalsifiable tolerance:null) and the Part 3.1b trivial-transcription framing. The new detail_evidence paragraphs provide the azimuthal-symmetry and live-panel attacks that the verdict summary alludes to without needing changes to the summary itself.
cross_section_propagation: No propagation required. Part 6 sections.json already contains the azimuthal-symmetry argument using the 'Sub-Terrestrial Wall' phrase — we have now VERIFIED that phrase is the dome's verbatim text (https://john09289.github.io/predictions/wins.html#win-064), so Part 6 needs no correction either. This expansion imports the Part 6 argument into the WIN panel where it was missing (primary fix for ISS-1273) and bidirectionally validates the existing Part 6 quote attribution.

monitoring_tag_note: The curmudgeon review flagged code_analysis.monitoring='none' as stale. It has since been flipped to 'hardcoded' in wins.json (already integrated before this EXP was written). Given the status_history.json inspection above, 'hardcoded' is a reasonable simplification — the PREDICTION string is static, the pass flag is effectively fixed, and the live-fetched OBSERVATION value is orthogonal to the prediction. A more precise schema would have a 'live_fetch_unrelated' or 'live_fetch_untested' tag, but introducing that is out of scope for this EXP. No change required to monitoring tag for now.
